hi.heading: उदाहरण
hi.content: Tumne kabhi apni mummy ko chai banate dekha hai? Ya garmiyon mein nimbu pani? Jab hum cheeni paani mein milate hain, woh gayab ho jaati hai na? Aisa isliye hota hai kyunki paani ke particles ke beech mein khaali jagah hoti hai, jahan cheeni ke particles jaakar adjust ho jaate hain. Socho — kitni kamaal ki baat hai! Agar particles ke beech jagah na hoti toh kya hota?

hi.heading: गलतफहमी
hi.wrong: Kai baar students sochte hain ki solid cheezon mein ya bahut thandi cheezon mein particles bilkul ruk jaate hain, hilte nahi.
hi.correct: Lekin yeh galat hai! Particles hamesha move karte rehte hain, chahe woh solid hi kyun na ho. Solids mein woh apni jagah par vibrate karte hain. Temperature kam hone se unki speed bas thodi kam ho jaati hai, rukte nahi. Agar particles bilkul ruk jaate toh kya cheezein apna roop badal paati?

hi.heading: गतिविधि
hi.content: Ek kaam karo — ek transparent glass mein saaf paani lo. Ab ek dropper se usme ink ki ek boond dalo aur use bilkul mat hilaana. Dekho — kya hota hai? Ink bina hilaye poore paani mein kaise fail gayi?

en.heading: Example
en.content: Have you ever seen your mom making chai, or maybe nimbu pani in summer? When we add sugar to water, it seems to disappear, right? This happens because there's empty space between the water particles, and the sugar particles fit right into those gaps. Socho zara — isn't that cool? What do you think would happen if there were no spaces between particles?

en.heading: Misconception
en.wrong: Many students believe that particles in solids or very cold substances stop moving completely.
en.correct: But that's not true! Particles are *always* in motion, even in solids (where they just vibrate in their fixed positions). Lower temperature only reduces their speed, they never completely stop. If particles completely stopped moving, would substances be able to change their state?

en.heading: Activity
en.content: Ek kaam karo — grab a transparent glass and some clean water. Now, carefully drop a single drop of ink into it using a dropper and don't stir it at all. Dekho — what do you observe? How did the ink spread throughout the water without you mixing it?

hi.heading: उदाहरण
hi.content: Dekho — jab hum market se ‘pure doodh’ khareedte hain, ya ghar par ‘shudh ghee’ ki baat karte hain — toh humare dimaag mein kya aata hai? Hum sochte hain ki usme koi milawat nahi hai, bilkul asli cheez hai. Lekin science ki nazar se dekho — kya tumhara doodh सच में एक शुद्ध पदार्थ (pure substance) है? Socho zara — doodh mein pani, fat, protein, sugar — kitni saari cheezein hoti hain! Toh science mein ‘pure’ ka matlab kuch aur hota hai, hai na? Ab socho, tumhare ghar mein aur kaun si cheez hai jo lagti toh pure hai par hoti mixture hai?

hi.heading: गलतफहमी
hi.wrong: अगर कोई चीज़ देखने में एक जैसी लगे (जैसे दूध या चीनी का घोल), तो वह एक शुद्ध पदार्थ (pure substance) है।
hi.correct: नहीं, science में 'pure' का मतलब है कि उस चीज़ में केवल एक ही प्रकार के कण (particles) हों। दूध देखने में भले ही एक जैसा लगता है, पर वो पानी, फैट, प्रोटीन का एक मिश्रण (mixture) है। इसी तरह, चीनी का घोल भी एक मिश्रण (mixture) है, शुद्ध पदार्थ (pure substance) नहीं।

hi.heading: गतिविधि
hi.content: Ek kaam karo — ek glass mein thoda pani lo aur usme ek chamach namak mila do. Dekhna, namak gayab ho jayega aur tumhe ek साफ घोल (clear solution) मिलेगा। Ab doosre glass mein pani lo aur usme thoda atta (flour) ya mitti mila kar dekho — क्या तुम्हें लगता है कि दोनों मिश्रण एक जैसे दिखेंगे?

en.heading: Example
en.content: Dekho — when we go to buy milk from the dairy, we often ask for 'pure milk', right? Or when we talk about 'shudh ghee' at home, we mean it’s unadulterated, free from mixing. But think about it — from a science perspective, is that milk *really* a pure substance? Socho zara — milk has water, fat, proteins, sugars — so many things mixed together! So, pure in science means something completely different, doesn't it? Now, can you think of any other thing at home that looks pure but is actually a mixture?

en.heading: Misconception
en.wrong: If something looks uniform or consistent, like milk or a sugar solution, then it's a pure substance.
en.correct: No, in science, 'pure' means a substance made up of only one type of particle. Milk, even though it looks uniform, is a mixture of water, fat, and proteins. Similarly, a sugar solution is a mixture, not a pure substance, even if it looks perfectly clear.

en.heading: Activity
en.content: Ek kaam karo — take a glass of water and mix a spoon of salt in it. You'll see the salt disappear, giving you a clear solution. Now, in another glass of water, try mixing a little atta (flour) or soil. Do you think both mixtures will look the same?
